title: Urban biodiversity arks
data_sources: IUCN Red List of Threatened Species v.2018-1

data_scope.spatial_coverage: Illustrative cities: Los Angeles, Cape Town, Austin, Chicago, San Francisco, Honolulu/Hilo, New Zealand, Sydney-Victoria coast; no systematic global dataset
data_scope.species_coverage: 392 living parrot species; 18 non-native parrot species with US breeding populations; 2 IUCN Endangered parrots in US urban areas
data_scope.article_type: Brief Communication / perspective — no new primary field data collected
data_scope.key_statistic: 109 of 392 parrot species listed as Threatened; 14 of 18 US non-native parrot species are Least Concern; 2 are Endangered

core_conclusion: Urban biodiversity is an unplanned species assemblage, yet cities already harbour IUCN-listed endangered species as wild, unmanaged non-native populations. Rather than passively waiting for chance colonizers to fill ecological niches humans create, cities could be deliberately managed as semi-natural assurance colonies for threatened species using ecological models, public-acceptability research, and urban planning — reducing extinction risk while enriching urban wildlife for residents.
why_it_matters: Reframes cities from biodiversity threats to potential refugia; challenges native-only conservation orthodoxy by showing IUCN-listed species survive better as non-natives in cities than in native ranges; proposes actionable policy shift from reactive tolerance to proactive planned introductions; bridges ecology, social science, and urban planning.
why_nature: Paradigm-shifting proposal for urban sustainability infrastructure; links human-built environments to extinction risk across human and natural systems; policy-translatable at city scale worldwide; timely under accelerating climate change that expands non-native invasion corridors.
added_value_to_my_research: GeoAI habitat suitability models using remote sensing + POI data operationalize the paper's call for predictive ecological models of urban ecosystems
15-minute-city accessibility metrics applied to urban nature patches measure biodiversity access equity — extending my R2SFCA framework to ecology
Spatial multi-criteria optimization for (city, species) ark site selection is structurally identical to my EV-charging facility-location work
Spatiotemporal kernel density estimation + multi-source data fusion (citizen science, social media, mobile-phone records) builds the monitoring system the paper lacks
MGWR/GWR of social-media sentiment about urban wildlife maps public acceptability spatially, feeding into equity-aware ark siting as a constraint layer

related.title: Urban biodiversity is affected by human-designed features of public squares
related.relation: shows which urban design variables drive species presence — inputs for ecological niche models

related.title: Resetting the clock by integrating urban nature and its biodiversity into the 15-minute city concept
related.relation: directly extends the ark concept by embedding biodiversity into the 15-minute-city accessibility framework

related.title: Global impacts of future urban expansion on terrestrial vertebrate diversity
related.relation: provides the global threat layer showing where habitat loss makes urban arks most urgent

related.title: Multidecadal legacy of uneven urbanization on divergent prospects for bird biodiversity
related.relation: demonstrates that historical urban legacy effects shape current biodiversity — motivates longitudinal ark monitoring

related.title: Historical redlining is associated with increasing geographical disparities in bird biodiversity sampling in the United States
related.relation: links urban social inequity to biodiversity data gaps — essential context for equitable ark siting

related.title: Promoting avian diversity in cities requires multiple parks with variable features
related.relation: empirically characterizes what urban park features drive bird species richness — directly informing ark habitat design

figures.caption: Red-crowned amazon parrots (Amazona viridigenalis) in Pasadena, California — an IUCN Endangered species thriving as a non-native wild population in urban Los Angeles
Gold-dust day gecko (Phelsuma laticauda) in Hilo, Hawaii — a Least Concern non-native that filled a niche that could have been occupied by a critically endangered Phelsuma species
